title: practical english usage - Part 152
content:
She arrived right after breakfast.

right Right with adverbials means 'just', 'exactly' or 'all the way'.

That was real nice. He cooks real well.

adjectives and adverbs.

real In informal American English, real is often used instead of really before I'll get back as quick(ly) as I can.

verbs of movement.

quick In an informal style, quick is often used instead of quickly, especially after I'm getting pretty fed up. Isn't your little girl dressed prettily?

Prettily means 'in a pretty way'. Compare: pretty The  informal adverb of degree pretty is similar to rather (►   460).

Adverbs     and   Adverbials              16 grammar •  195  adverb particles: up, down, back, away, etc adverb particles); from, during (only prepositions).

prepositions, but there are some exceptions: for example back, away (only up. Many words of this kind can be used as both adverb particles and below, by, down, forward, in, home, near, off, on, out, over, past, through, under, above, about, across, ahead, along, (a)round, aside, away, back, before, behind, Small adverbs like these are usually called 'adverb(ial) particles'. They  include They  are adverbs, not prepositions.

In sit down, go in and She's not up, the words down, in and up have no objects.

and up are prepositions: they have objects (the road, his office and my leg).

In the expressions down the road, in his office and up my leg, the words down, in You can go in.

- He's in his office.

She's not up yet.

- Something's climbing up my leg.

Please sit down.

- I ran down the road.

Words like down, in, up are not always prepositions. Compare: 1  adverb      particles      and   prepositions 195  adverb      particles:     up,   down,      back,     away,      etc He looked at me real strange. Think positive.

adverbs of manner.

In informal American English, many other adjective forms can also be used as 4 American      English Can you drive a bit slower? Let's see who can do it quickest.

comparatives and superlatives.

Informal uses of adjective forms as adverbs are especially common with 3 comparatives          and   superlatives You guessed wrong.

I wrongly believed that you wanted to help me.

wrong Wrong can be used informally instead of wrongly after a verb. Compare: Note also the expression wide awake (the opposite of fast asleep).

They have widely differing opinions.

The door was wide open. She's travelled widely.

wide The  normal adverb is wide; widely suggests distance or separation.

For details, ►    622.

well). Well is also an adjective meaning 'in good health' (the opposite of ill).

well Well is an adverb corresponding to the adjective good (a good singer sings grammar *196   position of adverbials: introduction However, not everybody agreed. Fortunately, nobody was hurt.

about) usually go in front position.

adverbials (which show a speaker's or writer's opinion of what he/she is talking Connecting adverbials (which join a clause to what came before) and comment 3  What    goes    where?

What are you doing tomorrow?

c  end   position      (at  the   end   of  a  clause) I have never understood her.

My brother completely forgot my birthday.

b  mid-position        (with   the   verb    - for   exact    details     ►    200) Yesterday morning something very strange happened.

a  front    position      (at  the   beginning       of  a  clause) There are three normal positions for adverbials: 2 front,    mid-   and   end   position Could you switch off the light?

But an adverb particle like on, off, out can go between a verb and a noun object.

She speaks English well. (     She speaks well English-.) verb + object + adverbial not / often get headaches. (     I get often headaches.) adverbial + verb + object We   do not usually put adverbials between a verb and its object.

1  verb    and   object:    She     speaks        English       well.

adverbials.

grammar, and it is impossible to give reliable rules that apply all the time to all ►     197-202 give some more detail. However, this is a very complicated area of The  very simple explanations below show the commonest patterns; entries Different kinds of adverbials typically go in different positions in a clause.
